Just look at the craziness that happened in China.  In April 2024, the government decided that influencers flaunting wealth should be completely banned.  So, they simply deleted all the content in one night.  4,700 posts deleted in two weeks, 383 accounts completely taken down .  Influencers with 4 million followers have disappeared.  A decade of work simply vanished completely.  If you don't know me, my name is Lincoln Fracá.  I lived in China for over 12 years, and what happened there is exactly what many people [in the music industry] who make a living online anywhere in the world, including here in Brazil, can't even imagine could happen.  In the next few minutes I'm going to tell you what happened, and why it changes everything you need to understand about working with the platform.  First of all, leave your like, don't forget to subscribe to the channel and activate the bell so you don't miss the next videos about Tin [music], these fantastic videos that I record here for you. On April 23, 2024, the Chinese government launched a campaign called Tin Llan, which means clean and bright.  It sounds like an innocent name, doesn't it?  But it isn't.  It was an operation against anyone who was flaunting their wealth on the internet.  The government looked at TikTok, which is the Chinese equivalent of TikTok, saw millions of videos of people showing off diamonds, rare gems, luxury bags, and said: "This is becoming social noise, we have to delete it." But here's the clever idea. Why did China do this specifically in April ?  Because in April of that year, China's retail sales grew by only 2.3%, the slowest growth since 2022. The economy was slowing down.  The real estate market had gone into decline, and the government understood something that most countries still haven't realized.  When millions of young people spend hours watching people flaunt luxury, they don't get inspired, they get frustrated, and they want to consume luxury too.  That's when money leaves the productive economy and goes to aspirational consumption, which doesn't generate real value for the economy.  At that time, China could not afford that kind of attitude.  It wasn't traditional censorship.  The government didn't come and say, "You've been banned for a crime." It was a product decision.  The owner of the platform, or whoever controls the platform, simply rewrote the rules.  By order of the government, obviously.  Din deleted 4,701 pieces of content and 11 accounts between the first and seventh day of May.  X R Hunchu, a platform that's like a mix of Instagram and TikTok, lost 383 points in two weeks.  The reprogrammed algorithm now automatically prevented any mention of Hermès, Porsche, expensive dinners, and five-star restaurants from delivering that content.  The machine did not amplify.  And here's a detail that many people ignore.  It was a product decision, not just a legal one.  The platforms have been placed in a legal position where they can be fined up to 100,000 euros, approximately R$77,000, for violations. In other words, it's not a matter of maybe having a problem; if there is, you'll pay for it.  The influencers who had built their entire careers on that algorithm woke up one day and had nothing left.  I'm going to tell you who those mega-influencers were and who they are now, and what they've lost.  W Home Chenin, the king of Chinese internet jewelry , nicknamed the Chinese Kim Kardashian , had 4.3 million followers. The strategy was simple: to appear covered in pink diamonds, rare jads, and pearls the size of eggs.  And he said he never left home wearing clothes worth less than eight of those a year, which is roughly R$ million per outfit.  His feed was basically "look how much money I have," and there are a lot of people here in Brazil on the same page. Wang was a character.  His personal trademark was being the richest man you knew.  The algorithm loved recommending Doin because people were mesmerized by the content. High engagement, very high revenue.  His account no longer exists; it simply disappeared. Young Master B, with nearly 3 million followers, was seen testing Porsches and showing off his Hermès collection.  He was restricted from gaining new followers on Din and banned from Eibo and Xunchu.  But here's the worst part: it wasn't deleted.  It still exists, but nobody sees it.  It's like being alive, but invisible.  Worse than dying digitally is becoming a ghost. Which one do you think is worse?  Will you lose your account if it's deleted or if no one else can see your content?  Bair J, a sister to Baloni ( named after the expensive mollusk), amassed 2 million followers by showcasing five-star dinners, mansion tours, and closets full of baby bags.  Your accounts became inaccessible.  These three had something in common.  They weren't educators, they didn't sell products, they had no skills, no abilities, they were symbols living off money.  The algorithm amplified them because the platform profited from that engagement, from people watching more videos.  But when the game's owner changed the rules, they had nowhere left to run.  They didn't have a real community, they didn't have a business, they didn't have intellectual property, they just had a contract.  And that contract was terminated.  Now comes the part that explains why nobody protested, made any noise.  The government didn't simply delete the accounts, make it seem like a natural oversight, rewrite the recommendation policy, and everyone thought: "Ah, this must be what the public wants to see instead of that other thing." The trend has become commonplace: cooking at home.  Simple apartment, patriotic content.  The influencers haven't changed their values.  No one woke up feeling humble. They changed because the money left one side and went into the other.  Even here in Brazil, we're catching those videos of a very simple person, in a simple house, cooking at home.  I've seen a few, I don't know if you've seen them around.  Now imagine, you were making 10 million a month just from showing off.  The algorithm changes, your videos don't go viral, and your revenue disappears.   Do you want to win again?  The format changes, and the content becomes simple and authentic.  Some obviously couldn't manage it and disappeared along with that old style.  Others reinvented themselves in days.  In one week, the entire platform's standard changed.  A well-organized apartment has become aspirational, not a mansion.  But here's a disturbing detail.  After Crackdown, influencers started pretending to be UN delegates, renting office space at the UN and posing for photos.  Others pretend to be married to wealthy Arab princes in order to sell counterfeit goods.  When you push the creativity of lying to the limit, you end up finding another way.  But now comes the part that's too important to let pass.  For a decade, you work for 10 years building a personal brand, million-dollar contracts, millions of followers; it seems permanent, like it's yours, that nobody can take it away.  All of this can disappear with a single policy update or through the platform, without anyone notifying them.  And that's the point. Platform ownership is a rental agreement, not an asset.  Have you ever heard the saying, "Don't build your house in someone else's family"?  That's pretty much it , but for the platform.  The property is yours.  You build a house, it's yours for life.   In a physical store, you are the owner.  A trademark is one that no one can take away.  Now, whether it's a platform or a marketplace, you're renting space.  And whoever controls the platform, whether it's a company or the government, can change the rules whenever they want.  They can raise the rent, they can cancel the contract, they can decide that what you're doing now is illegal or against policies that weren't wrong before.  China didn't invent it, but it was the first to do it on a large scale. Think about it, in one week China rewrote what is beautiful, what is elegant, what is aspirational.  Millions of people woke up and looked at the Fed. Suddenly, what they wanted to be changed.  Not because they changed their minds, but because the machine said that something else was now in fashion.  This means that you, who watch internet content and are inspired by someone who has a certain standard of what success is, are using a compass that isn't your own.  And when that compass turns, you end up turning with it.  But there's more.  This isn't just an influencer problem; it's a problem for anyone who builds a business on the platform.   If you have a store on TikTok, you can turn it into a shop .  Selling on Instagram can make it disappear.  You have an audience on YouTube, the algorithm changes, and your viewership drops by 80%.  The risk is no longer theoretical, at least not in China.   It really happened and it can happen again. Could it be somewhere else in the world?   A platform is not an asset, it's a rental. You may totally disagree with this attitude, you may think it's totalitarian, crazy, but man, depending on what young people consume today, some people are losing control. Sometimes people do the wrong thing just to try out a different brand of clothing because they saw a celebrity wearing it. Or does this revert to a theory of dictatorship where we're forcing people not to use drugs when they are free to do so?  To what extent are we free to declare, speak, and influence whatever we want?  Is there a different weighting or method for evaluating these major influencers? [música] Em 2 de setembro de 2021, a Administração Nacional de Rádio e Televisão da China publicou um documento de oito pontos com título burocrático, notificação para fortalecer adicionalmente a gestão de programas culturais e seus profissionais. O conteúdo, no entanto, era outra coisa. Dentro desse documento, havia uma instrução direta às emissoras que colocassem fim aos Nianal e as outras estéticas anormais. Niampau é um termo pejorativo em mandarim que numa tradução aberta significa um xingamento de pátio de escola sendo usado pelo braço regulador do estado. Mas o documento não parava por aí, porque junto com os pau, o mesmo pacote bia reality shows de votação em ídolos, limitava salários celebridades, proibia a admiração pública por riqueza fácil e ostentação e ainda mandava as emissoras pararem de promover quem tivesse posições políticas incorretas. E no lugar de tudo isso, uma lista do que deveria aparecer no ar. cultura tradicional chinesa, cultura revolucionária, cultura socialista avançada. E o que os jovens chineses estavam querendo se tornar tava saindo do controle. Agora, o que exatamente estava saindo do controle e quem percebeu isso primeiro? Eu já vou chegar lá. Para entender o que o estado chinês quis eliminar, você precisa entender o que que ele tava vendo. Os Shao Shin Roll, literalmente os carninhas frescas, são jovens celebridades masculinas que emergiram por volta aí de 2014 com pele de porcelana, traços [música] delicados, todos fortemente inspirados pelos ídolos do K-pop, o Jpop. Para comparar, a conta oficial do Partido Comunista no Ebô nunca nem chegou perto disso. Esse era o tamanho da lealdade que esses ídolos comandavam entre os jovens. Um governo obsecado com estabilidade viu isso uma preocupação que ia muito além da estética. [música] Em agosto de 2021, quando o cantor Chris Whoo foi preso por suspeita de estupro, seus fãs organizaram online um plano de operação de resgate para tirar da prisão. Ninguém foi às ruas, mas o sinal estava dado um grupo de jovens dispostos a desafiar a autoridade do estado em nome de um ídolo de entretenimento. Para o Partido Comunista Chinês, isso não é uma curiosidade sociológica, é alarme de incêndio. Em tudo, antes de qualquer proibição, o partido tentou outra coisa, recrutar esses mesmos rostos para o seu próprio projeto. E o que aconteceu com essa tentativa é a parte mais interessante de toda essa história. Voltamos a 2017, quando o governo encomendou um épico bélico para celebrar o nonagéso aniversário do exército de libertação popular, a fundação de um exército, um filme de um herói nacional e para quem escalaram os papéis dos mais venerados comandantes militares da história comunista chinesa, Luan L. Ma e Zangin. Quase metade do elenco era formado pelos mesmos rostos andrógenos que seriam banidos 4 anos depois. E pensem no que isso significa. Além disso, descendentes de outros generais históricos assinaram uma carta aberta chamando casting de vergonhoso e de nilismo histórico. O estado havia tentado recrutar a estética dos ídolos pro seu projeto, mas a estética dos ídolos acabou contaminando o próprio projeto. A diretriz, as multas, os rostos banidos, tudo isso tem uma origem comum. E essa origem nos leva a um homem que em 1988 atravessou os Estados Unidos no ônibus e voltou com uma conclusão que mudaria toda a estratégia cultural da China por décadas. Um jovem professor de ciência política chamado Juan Runin embarca num avião paraos Estados Unidos e ao longo de 6 meses ele visita 30 cidades e 20 universidades. Berkley, Harvard, Iowa, Chicago. Ele acompanha de perto as campanhas presenciais, passa tempo no Congresso e conversa com os think tanks como a Brook Institution e volta a escrever um livro chamado América contra América. A tese não é que os Estados Unidos são ruins, mas sim que os Estados Unidos carregam dentro da própria estrutura as forças que vão destruí-los, a individualidade excessiva, a mercantilização de tudo e a erosão de valores coletivos pela indústria do entretenimento. E a observação que mais importa para o que estamos discutindo é essa. Conforme Juan Runin concluiu, uma civilização não cai no campo de batalha, ela cai pela mídia, pelo entretenimento, pela narrativa que circula na cabeça das pessoas. Hoje, Wan Runin é membro do comitê permanente do Polit Bureau, o mais alto órgão decisório da China, quarto na hierarquia e arquiteto ideológico de três presidentes consecutivos, Janzemin, Rudinal e Shidinpin.
